name: Sonata Form (First Movement)
description: Classical sonata-allegro form with two contrasting themes, development, and recapitulation

sections.name: Exposition - Primary Theme
sections.description: Strong, assertive opening theme establishing the home key
sections.melody_character: Bold, rhythmically distinctive theme with a clear motif (short memorable pattern of 2-4 notes). Strong rhythmic profile. Often starts with an upbeat or anacrusis. Should be immediately memorable and recognizable when it returns.
sections.accompaniment_character: Supportive, reinforcing the rhythmic energy. Can be unison passages, block chords, or tremolo.
sections.harmonic_rhythm: Moderate (1-2 chords per bar), strong tonal center
sections.ends_with: Transition passage modulating to dominant (or relative major if minor key)

sections.name: Exposition - Secondary Theme
sections.description: Contrasting lyrical theme in a new key
sections.key_relation: dominant (V) if tonic is major; relative major (III) if tonic is minor
sections.melody_character: Lyrical, singing quality - contrasts with the rhythmic Primary Theme. Longer note values, smoother contour. May include a closing theme (codetta) that is more rhythmic.
sections.accompaniment_character: Lighter texture, possibly arpeggiated or with a singing bass line
sections.ends_with: Strong cadence in the new key, possibly with a brief codetta

sections.name: Development
sections.description: Fragmentation and transformation of both themes through various keys
sections.melody_character: CRITICAL: Must use recognizable fragments of both Primary and Secondary themes, but transformed: inverted, augmented, diminished, sequenced through different keys. Build tension through fragmentation (shorter and shorter motif statements). Include a retransition (dominant pedal) preparing the recapitulation.
sections.accompaniment_character: More contrapuntal, themes may appear in bass or inner voices. Increased textural complexity.
sections.harmonic_rhythm: Fast and unstable, frequent modulations, sequences
sections.ends_with: Dominant pedal (sustained or repeated V chord) building tension before recapitulation

sections.name: Recapitulation - Primary Theme
sections.description: Return of Primary Theme in the home key
sections.melody_character: CRITICAL: Must be the SAME melody as Exposition Primary Theme, possibly with minor variations. The return should feel like a homecoming.
sections.accompaniment_character: Same as exposition, possibly slightly fuller
sections.ends_with: Transition, but this time staying in the tonic key

sections.name: Recapitulation - Secondary Theme
sections.description: Secondary Theme now transposed to the home key
sections.key_relation: tonic (NOT dominant - this is the key difference from exposition)
sections.melody_character: CRITICAL: Same melody as Exposition Secondary Theme but transposed to the tonic key. This resolves the tonal tension of the exposition.
sections.accompaniment_character: Same character as exposition secondary theme
sections.ends_with: Strong perfect authentic cadence in tonic

sections.name: Coda
sections.description: Final closing section affirming the tonic
sections.melody_character: Based on Primary Theme motif, triumphant character. Strong rhythmic drive to the final cadence.
sections.harmonic_rhythm: Slow, emphasis on tonic and dominant
sections.ends_with: Multiple perfect authentic cadences, final tonic chord

composition_rules: Primary and Secondary themes must have clearly different characters (rhythmic vs lyrical)
Exposition Secondary Theme must be in dominant or relative major
Recapitulation Secondary Theme MUST be transposed to tonic
Development must use material from both themes, not new material
Retransition (end of Development) must create strong expectation for tonic return
Primary Theme motif should be short (2-4 notes) and immediately recognizable
